description: The following recommendations should be considered while configuring the IP addresses for primary and backup controllers: While configuring a primary-controller ip <ipv4-address>, it is recommended to use the IP address of a standalone controller or an IP address of a controller in the primary cluster. Similarly, while configuring a backup-controller ip <ipv4-address>, it is recommended to use the IP address of a standalone controller or an IP address of a controller in the backup cluster. Configuring both primary or backup controller IP addresses from the same cluster is not supported.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.14.1060, what caveat is documented for User Based Tunnel?

Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - The following recommendations should be considered while configuring the IP addresses for primary and backup controllers: While configuring a primary-controller ip <ipv4-address>, it is recommended to use the IP address of a standalone controller or an IP address of a controller in the primary cluster. Similarly, while configuring a backup-controller ip <ipv4-address>, it is recommended to use the IP address of a standalone controller or an IP address of a controller in the backup cluster. Configuring both primary or backup controller IP addresses from the same cluster is not supported.

description: User-Based Tunneling (UBT) and DHCPv4/v6 snooping are mutually exclusive features in AOS-CX. When these two features are configured simultaneously, binding entries for the end clients will not be populated in the binding database. IP source-lockdown feature permits traffic from the clients to pass through the switch only if the client's MAC and IP addresses are present in the binding database. IP source lockdown can be enabled at both port and VLAN levels. The port-level configuration will be overridden by the VLAN-level configuration only when the port is a member of a single VLAN. If the port is a member of multiple VLANs, then the VLAN-level configuration for IP source lockdown will not be applied. Client IP tracker can track the IP address until the IP source lockdown is applied. Once the lockdown is in effect, the IP address will only be tracked if there is a valid entry in the binding table. However, if an IP address is already being tracked, it will remain active through probes, as probes utilize ARP packets, which are Layer 2 (L2) packets rather than Layer 3 (L3) packets.

When these two features are configured simultaneously, binding entries for the end clients will not be populated in the binding database. IP source lockdown can be enabled at both port and VLAN levels. The port-level configuration will be overridden by the VLAN-level configuration only when the port is a member of a single VLAN. description: When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.

To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.

description: The switch does not support double encapsulation. A packet can be encapsulated with either L2GRE (UBT) or VXLAN, but not both. The network admin should decide the tunneling type to be used, and then plan the configuration accordingly.

description: In the event of license issues when onboarding a DUT to primary or backup mobility conductor, the DUT will not try to bootstrap to another mobility conductor where a license is available. For example, if a mobility conductor does not have a license to onboard the DUT but the backup mobility conductor does have adequate licenses, if both mobility conductors are reachable then UBT will be down, and the DUT will not attempt to bootstrap to the backup controller. However, if the primary mobility conductor is not reachable, the DUT gets tunneled to the standby/backup mobility conductor. Once the primary mobility conductor is reachable by the DUT once again, the DUT will not automatically bootstrap back to the primary. Network administrators should manually disable and enable UBT on the DUT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary mobility conductor.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.14.1060, what additional caveat is documented for User Based Tunnel?

Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - In the event of license issues when onboarding a DUT to primary or backup mobility conductor, the DUT will not try to bootstrap to another mobility conductor where a license is available. However, if the primary mobility conductor is not reachable, the DUT gets tunneled to the standby/backup mobility conductor. Once the primary mobility conductor is reachable by the DUT once again, the DUT will not automatically bootstrap back to the primary. Use UBT vlan-extend mode to support multiple MAC address or clients in a differently tagged VLAN on a single switchport.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.14.1060, what caveat is documented for UBT Design?

Use UBT vlan-extend mode to support multiple MAC address or clients in a differently tagged VLAN on a single switchport.

description: Symptom: Packets near MTU size limits may not be present in a port or tunnel/remote based mirror. Scenario: When a customer configures a mirror where the destination interface has the same MTU size, mirrored packets that are close to the maximum MTU are incorrectly dropped and are not mirrored. This also applies to remote/tunnel mirrors. Workaround: Configure mirror destination ports as routing port and set the ip mtu size. For a VLAN interface, set the ip mtu on the VLAN SVI. IP MTU must be set to accommodate the ethernet overhead +tag. For the default MTU, 1500, the ip mtu would be 1518. For a tunneled scenario, the MTU must also account for the tunnel header.

description: Symptom: The port-accessd daemon repeatedly crashes when 802.1X clients attempt to authenticate on the switch. This results in authentication failures for both 802.1X and MAC Authentication Bypass (MAB) clients. The system logs show repeated crash messages such as the following: port-accessd crashed due to signal:11. Scenario: When the RADIUS server is configured to return a Session-Timeout attribute with value set to 0 and an 802.1X client attempts to authenticate on the switch, the port-accessd daemon repeatedly crashes. Workaround: Remove the Session-Timeout = 0 attribute from the RADIUS server configuration or configure a value greater than 0. Use the client inactivity timeout none in the role configuration instead.

description: Symptom: clear interface statistics global does not clear the counters on 6200M platforms. Scenario: clear interface statistics global is accepted by the system, but fails to reset interface statistics on 6200M switches running in a Virtual Switching Framework (VSF) topology. While the command appears to execute successfully with no error messages displayed, interface counters remain unchanged after execution. Workaround: Use clear interface statistics without the global parameter.

description: Symptom: After a virtual machine is migrated (vMotion) from one data center to another in a static VXLAN environment, the VM cannot reach its gateway or external destinations, resulting in ping timeouts and connection failures. Scenario: In some scenarios, the switch can fail to program tunnel to VNI associations, leading to traffic loss. Workaround: Remove the affected VNI from the configuration on the switches in the source data center and then add the VNI to the configuration again.

description: Symptom: With hpe-relay core dump legacy bootp client does not get IP. Scenario: When the DHCP relay daemon processes a legacy BOOTP client packet that lacks the option-53 field, it fails to properly validate the packet's structure before attempting to access this field, leading to a crash. This issue occurs exclusively when the smart-relay feature is enabled. Workaround: Disable the global DHCP smart-relay configuration if not required using no dhcp-smart-relay.

description: Symptom: When switching between IPTV channels, there is an intermittent delay of 2-6 seconds before the new video stream is displayed to the end user. Scenario: The system experiences a small delay in the program of a new multicast flow after receiving the IGMP report. During this time, critical initial video packets containing SPS (Sequence Parameter Set) and PPS (Picture Parameter Set) information may be dropped. These packets are essential for the decoder to properly display the H.264 video stream. Workaround: Configure static IGMP groups for the multicast streams using static igmp join <multicast-group-address> interface <decoder-port>. This ensures the hardware is pre-programmed with the multicast flows, eliminating the join latency.

description: Symptom: When uploading firmware using the Web UI or REST API, the system displays the following error message despite the firmware being successfully transferred to the device: There was an error uploading new firmware. Firmware image is invalid. Please enter a valid firmware image. Scenario: When logging console is enabled in the switch configuration and a user attempts to upload firmware through the Web UI or REST API, the upload process is completed, but an HTTP 500 error is generated. Workaround: Until upgrading to the fixed version, use one of the following methods: 1. Disable console logging by removing logging console from the configuration. Then attempt the firmware upload using the Web UI or REST API again. 2. Use TFTP for firmware upload by uploading the firmware using TFTP or SFTP instead of the Web UI or REST API. Example command: copy tftp/sftp://[server-ip]/[firmware-file] primary vrf mgmt.

description: Symptom: In Aruba Central Network (CNX), the application total usage statistics displayed for a wired client are significantly higher than the interface-level total usage statistics for the same client. Scenario: After a VSF switchover event, the IPFIX flow counters reported to Aruba Central become inconsistent with the actual interface statistics. For example, the application usage might show thousands of packets while the interface statistics show only hundreds.

description: Symptom: When wireless clients roam between access points connected to the same switch, they lose connectivity and receive an APIPA address. Connectivity is only restored after the MAC-age timer expires. Scenario: When a client is authenticated with a VLAN on a secure port, the VLAN is added as an ingress-only VLAN on other secure ports, but the MSTP state for those ports remains in blocked state instead of forwarding state, preventing seamless client movement. Workaround: Reduce the MAC-age timer to minimize the impact. or configure AP ports in device-mode instead of client-mode as follows: interface 1/1/X port-access client-limit 1 port-access client-mode device

description: Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.
